zotero.abstractNote: Metacognition—the ability to monitor one's own performance—is a cornerstone of human cognition. While various measures of metacognition exist, they often lack a common theoretical foundation, making it difficult to compare results across studies. We propose a framework based on information theory to quantify metacognition. We define metacognition as the mutual information between a person's confidence and their performance. We show that this measure is related to the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) and the area under the confidence-accuracy characteristic curve (AUCAUC). We derive upper and lower bounds for this information-theoretic measure and show that it is related to the performance of a group of agents. Our framework provides a unified perspective on metacognition and allows for a principled comparison of different measures.
